data.title: Dollar Recovers as Tariff Uncertainty Eases — WSJ
data.description:
By Peter Landers

The U.S. dollar, which took a dive against global currencies after President Trump's "liberation day" tariff announcement in April, is recovering some ground this week. As investors get a clearer picture of Trump's tariffs-including a common 15% rate for major trading partners such as the European Union, Japan and South Korea-doubts about the dollar's longstanding status as the main global reserve currency have ebbed for now. The Federal Reserve's decision this week to keep rates steady also lifted the dollar.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Capstone Copper Corp reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Capstone Copper Corp reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 4 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended June 30, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 3 cents. The mean expectation of fifteen analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 5 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 2 cents to 12 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 38.2% to $543.20 million from a year ago; analysts expected $561.05 million.

  • Capstone Copper Corp's reported EPS for the quarter was 3 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $24 million.

  • Capstone Copper Corp shares had fallen by 9.9% this quarter and lost 15.3% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 0.3% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, five analysts negatively revised earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 16 "strong buy" or "buy," 1 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the specialty mining & metals peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Capstone Copper Corp is C$12.00, about 35.3% above its last closing price of C$7.77

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data August 1 at 04:11 a.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: Asia Stock Markets Fall as Trump Sets New Tariffs for Dozens of Countries
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

Asia-Pacific equity markets fell broadly, following U.S. stock futures lower, after President Trump announced steeper tariffs against dozens of countries while delaying the effective date by a week.

The U.S. will raise tariffs on scores of nations, effective at 12:01 a.m. on Aug. 7, according to an executive order Trump signed on Thursday. When the action is taken, the result will be significantly higher duties on virtually every U.S. trading partner.

Trump has set a 15% tariff with major industrialized economies including the European Union, Japan and South Korea.

Other nations with which the U.S. has a small trade deficit would also get 15% tariffs, a senior administration official told reporters. Countries with which the U.S. runs a trade surplus would be hit with 10% tariffs, the official said

Taiwan's Taiex fell 0.7% on Friday, while Japan's Nikkei Stock Average was 0.4% lower. South Korea's Kospi dropped 3.3% and Australia's S&P/ASX 200 shed 0.8%.

Futures tied to U.S. indexes edged lower. Nasdaq and S&P 500 futures were down about 0.2% each, while Dow futures lost 0.1%.

The Trump administration's decision gives nations another week to finalize trade deals that could lower their levies. The U.S. has secured agreements with a number of countries in recent weeks, including major trading partners.

Tariff-related developments will likely "remain fluid in the coming weeks," DBS economists Radhika Rao and Chua Han Teng said in a note.

The tariffs were expected for Asia, with larger export countries reasonably facing lower duties than Southeast Asian nations, said Lorraine Tan, director of equity research for Asia at Morningstar. Markets will likely shrug off this news, she said in a note. "The main focus is China, where negotiations are ongoing."

Trading was choppy in Hong Kong, with the Hang Seng Index wavering between slight gains and losses. The benchmark index was recently down 0.2%. China's Shanghai Composite Index was also 0.2% lower.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: SNG: Saudi, Russian energy ministers discuss oil market and joint committee plans
data.description:
First published: 01-Aug-2025 08:14:20

Staff Writer

RIYADH — Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman and Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak held a meeting in Riyadh to discuss recent developments in the global oil market and ongoing cooperation between the two countries within the OPEC+ alliance.

As co-chairs of the Saudi-Russian Joint Governmental Committee on Trade, Economic, Scientific, and Technical Cooperation, the two officials also reviewed progress since the committee’s eighth session, including the launch of direct flights between Saudi Arabia and Russia and the signing of multiple memorandums of understanding in fields such as industry, education, media, and Hajj and Umrah.

The meeting also covered ways to boost bilateral trade and expand economic collaboration.

Both sides discussed the upcoming ninth session of the joint committee, scheduled to be held in Riyadh on November 6, 2025.

According to a statement from the Saudi Ministry of Energy, the ministers reaffirmed the importance of coordination on oil production policy under OPEC+.

Russian news agency Interfax reported that Novak and Prince Abdulaziz discussed oil market trends and the outlook for deeper cooperation between the two energy producers.

Their meeting followed Monday’s virtual session of the OPEC+ Joint Ministerial Monitoring Committee (JMMC), which called for full compliance with output agreements. 

A separate meeting among eight OPEC+ members is expected this Sunday to decide on a potential oil production increase for September.

Goldman Sachs has forecasted that OPEC+ will announce a final production hike of around 550,000 barrels per day for September.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0310 GMT - Palm oil rises in early Asian trade amid bargain hunting after recent declines. Indian buyers may step in on price dips, especially if crude palm oil futures fall to around 4,150 ringgit ahead of the festive season, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Technically, CPO futures remain weak and any rebound may offer a shorting opportunity, it adds. AmInvestment Bank expects CPO to find support at 4,190 ringgit a ton and face resistance at 4,298 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives October contract rises 11 ringgit to 4,241 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0308 GMT - Iron ore prices are higher in early Asian trading, after falling sharply in the previous session. China's official manufacturing purchasing managers index extended its fall below 50 in July, highlighting the country's slowing production, ANZ Research says in a note. Meanwhile, China's home sales extended a slump in July despite declining prices, weighing on demand for the steel-making material. China's new focus on reducing disorderly competition and overcapacity could provide some support for the ferrous metal. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 0.5% higher at CNY788.5 a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0154 GMT - Productivity and simplification are key for Rio Tinto's next CEO, Simon Trott, to "return Rio to its former glory," Macquarie analysts say in a note. The analysts say Rio Tinto's minerals arm, which includes commodities such as titanium dioxide, borates and diamonds, undid all the good work of its copper division in 1H. "We think a portfolio simplification mantra could deliver value via reducing earnings volatility and risk." Macquarie raises its target on Rio's Australian shares by 3% to A$109/share. The stock is up 0.6% at A$112.32. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0143 GMT - A rebased commodity-price graph in Rio Tinto's 1H result shows copper and aluminum trading around long-term averages, but iron ore and lithium trading below. That prompts Macquarie analysts to contemplate the possible price risks it could face in those industries. "For those with long memories, they may recall when iron ore struggled to exceed $40/ton (nominal) prior to 2005 and lump VIU's [value-in-use] drove slim producer profits," say the analysts. At that time, the iron-ore market was experiencing oversupply from new basins, volume-based industry rivalry and strong customer buying power, they say. "We hope history does not repeat itself." (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0133 GMT - Base metals are mixed in the early Asian session. Copper prices are volatile after the Trump administration said refined copper, the most imported form of the metal to the U.S., would be excluded from tariffs, say Sucden Financial analysts. The U.S. granting a 90-day trade deal extension to Mexico, one of its largest aluminum sources, could also affect prices. The analysts reckon that copper's volatility could drive broader weakness across the base metals complex. The three-month LME copper contract is 0.2% higher at $9,626/ton, lead is 0.3% lower, and aluminum is up 0.2%. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2337 GMT - Gold is steady early in Asia as traders eye tariff-deal developments. President Trump announced a 90-day extension for Mexico, but White House Press Secretary Leavitt said the country is still facing U.S. sectoral tariffs. Meanwhile, Trump signed an executive order raising tariffs on Canada to 35% from 25%, the White House said. The coming hours will reveal whether we will see agreements that further diminish concerns about the trade war or whether we are entering a new phase of heightened fears over inflation risks and elevated interest rates, says Samer Hasn, senior market analyst at XS.com, in an email. Spot gold is little changed at $3,290.67/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2256 GMT [Dow Jones]--Global softwood kraft pulp market conditions are expected to stay weak in the third quarter, Canfor Pulp Products said. "The subdued demand for bleached kraft paper in North America observed at the end of the first quarter and throughout the second quarter is anticipated to persist into the third quarter," Canfor Pulp said. The outlook is mostly attributed to ongoing uncertainties related to Canada-US trade relations, and general global economic pressures, the company added. Purchasing activity, especially from China, is expected to remain soft during the traditionally slower summer period, Canfor said, adding "as a result, global pulp producer inventories are forecast to remain well above the balanced range through the third quarter of 2025." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

2001 GMT - A 90-day delay in the implementation of new tariffs on Mexican goods by the U.S. pressured live cattle futures, with the most-active contract closing down 2.8% to $2.23575 a pound. The drop-off came as traders extended a pullback seen this morning. With Mexico a key source of feeder cattle for a net import beef market, the fate of trade relations between the two nations is a key driver for how futures move. Lean hog futures close up 0.4% to 89.575 cents a pound. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1929 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures settle higher in a choppy session. A pickup in LNG feedgas demand and a still "quite hot" second week of August likely contributed to gains, NatGasWeather.com says in a note. "It could also simply be about bulls defending $3," the forecaster adds. The EIA reported a 48 Bcf storage build for last week, which was double the five-year average for the week, increasing the surplus to 195 Bcffrom 171 Bcf. Nymex natural gas settles up 2% at $3.106/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1928 GMT - Oil futures snap a three-day winning streak with the approach of the U.S. tariff deadline and the OPEC+ meeting that's expected to bring another large output increase for September. A rash of U.S. trade deals, and threats of Russia sanctions have oil prices on track for solid weekly gains. If the sanctions are avoided, "if it's business as usual, the fundamentals for oil should weaken," says Neil Crosby, of Sparta Commodities. "A lot of people are expecting weaker demand going forward, OPEC are due to export probably a lot more oil after summer, and with those two things combined you should start to see more of these inventory builds." WTI settles down 1.1% at $69.26 a barrel. September Brent goes off the board at $72.53, down 1%. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1829 GMT - Corn and wheat caught a boost in part from higher U.S. tariffs on Mexican goods being delayed for another 90 days. But soybeans remain down 0.7% for the day on expectations of a bigger-than-expected Midwestern crop. "The market is probing the downside amid large U.S. crop potential," says AgResource in a note. Many estimates for 2025 soybean yield will emerge in August. Corn is up 0.4%, but wheat is now virtually flat. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1700 GMT - Research from North Dakota State University, done in partnership with the Department of Agriculture's Agricultural Marketing Service, shows that ports globally have adequate capacity for shipping wheat exports, but the ports of leading wheat exporters Russia and Ukraine have, at times, only limited shipping capacity depending on the time of year. But should Russia invest more into its shipping infrastructure, that could dramatically impact the amount of wheat that ships out of the U.S., says the study. "Expanding Russian port infrastructure in the Baltic and Caspian Sea regions results in a reduction of U.S. exports of between 550,000 to 875,000 metric tons per year, depending on the degree of expansion," the study says. CBOT wheat is down 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: EMEA Morning Briefing: Trump Hits More Countries With Higher Tariffs
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

PMIs for eurozone, France, Germany, U.K.; EU inflation; trading updates for AXA, IAG, Pearson, Intertek Group, IMI, Engie, AngloGold Ashanti

Opening Call:

European stock futures were lower after Asia stocks broadly fell. The dollar was flat. Gold fell and oil was steady.

Equities:

European stock futures fell after U.S. President Donald Trump announced steeper levies for dozens of countries and stuck to his Friday deadline for imposing higher tariffs on Canada, while giving a one-week delay to other trading partners that had received letters.

Canada didn't come to an agreement and now will get 35% tariffs, with an exemption for goods compliant with the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement. But 18 other countries that also didn't make deals are due to get their higher tariffs in seven days.

Dozens of other countries that weren't among the 25 trading partners that got letters look set to have higher tariffs imposed on them on Aug. 7. Those nations include Switzerland, which faces a 39% import tax, along with Taiwan at 20%. Cameroon, Iceland, New Zealand and Norway are among the many countries looking at a 15% rate.

"People are coalescing around the idea that the economy is starting to slow," said Brent Schutte, chief investment officer at Northwestern Mutual Wealth Management. "The one thing holding up the stock market is the earnings of those tech giants."

Forex:

The Swiss franc weakened as Switzerland is poised to be one of the countries that will be hit hardest by U.S. tariffs.

The Swiss franc could face selling pressure given risks to Swiss exports to the U.S., which are dominated by pharmaceuticals, said DBS Group Research's Chang Wei Liang.

However, U.S.-Switzerland trade talks are still continuing, and "markets may be careful to watch headlines over a possible deal to lower tariffs, " the FX and credit strategist said.

Bonds:

Bond markets have priced in another Fed hold in September, according to CME, after Chair Powell's hawkish tone, while there is enough time for indicators to change the outcome.

"The Fed and Powell will continue to be data dependent," said Nuveen's Anders Persson. Indicators have been volatile, with Persson expecting U.S. payrolls to print just above 100,000, down from June's 147,000, with unemployment at 4.2%.

August's report will come before the next Fed meeting and the impact of immigration on labor supply remains unclear, he added, noting that his base-case scenario is two interest rate cuts this year, while acknowledging that "there are some crosswinds."

Energy:

Oil edged higher, supported by supply-disruption concerns.

President Trump gave Russian President Putin an Aug. 8 deadline to reach a cease-fire with Ukraine, shortening a 50-day deadline he had set previously.

This deadline for Russia to end its invasion and a full cease-fire has aided some worries over supply disruptions, said Janiv Shah, vice president at Rystad Energy.

Metals:

Gold was steady as traders eyed tariff-deal developments, as President Trump announced a 90-day extension for Mexico, but White House Press Secretary Leavitt said the country is still facing U.S. sectoral tariffs.

The coming hours will reveal whether we will see agreements that further diminish concerns about the trade war or whether we are entering a new phase of heightened fears over inflation risks and elevated interest rates, said Samer Hasn, senior market analyst at XS.com.

-

Base metals were mixed, with copper prices volatile after the Trump administration said refined copper, the most imported form of the metal to the U.S., would be excluded from tariffs, said Sucden Financial analysts.

The U.S. granting a 90-day trade deal extension to Mexico, one of its largest aluminum sources, could also affect prices.

The analysts said copper's volatility could drive broader weakness across the base metals complex.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Trump hits dozens more countries with higher tariffs that start Aug. 7

President Donald Trump late Thursday announced steeper levies for dozens of countries. He also stuck to his Friday deadline for imposing higher tariffs on Canada that he set in a letter to the U.S. neighbor, while giving a one-week delay to other trading partners that had received letters.

In the first half of July, 25 U.S. trading partners received letters from Trump that promised higher import taxes starting this Friday unless those countries made new trade deals with his administration.

Tariffs, Economic Concerns Drag Down Stocks

Rising anxiety about the economic outlook weighed on stock indexes Thursday, offsetting big gains for two large tech companies.

Meta Platforms shares soared 11%, while Microsoft jumped 3.9%, after both companies reported strong earnings late Wednesday. The results showed off the robustness of the two tech giants' existing businesses as they spend huge sums building out their artificial intelligence capabilities.

Big Tech's $400 Billion AI Spending Spree Just Got Wall Street's Blessing

The AI arms race is accelerating as the major tech companies add to their already gargantuan levels of spending. The bets are paying off for investors, but not for all employees.

Alphabet's Google, Microsoft, Amazon and Meta Platforms are set to spend nearly $400 billion this year on capital expenditures, largely to build their artificial-intelligence infrastructure. That is more than the European Union spent on defense last year.

How a Youth-Led Backlash Pushed Zelensky to His Biggest U-Turn Yet

KYIV, Ukraine-Anton Tymoshenko, a popular Ukrainian comedian, pressed into the protest crowd here last week holding a speaker above his head playing the voice of another humorist-the one who is now Ukraine's president.

If you encounter corruption, President Volodymyr Zelensky said in the 2019 message playing on Tymoshenko's speaker, call the National Anticorruption Bureau of Ukraine.

Bayer Ups Guidance After Pharmaceuticals Unit Outperforms

Bayer raised it sales and earnings guidance on a better-than-expected first half of the year for its pharmaceuticals business.

The German pharmaceutical and agricultural conglomerate on Thursday said it now expects to post revenue on a currency-adjusted basis of EUR46 billion, or the equivalent of $52.51 billion, to EUR48 billion for the year, up EUR1 billion on either end from its previous forecast.

Apple's iPhone Sales Blow Past Estimates as Customers Raced to Beat Tariffs

Apple iPhone sales blew past Wall Street's expectations in the June quarter as some U.S. consumers rushed to buy their devices before potential price increases from tariffs.

The robust sales growth for the company's signature product was the highest increase in years, exceeding 13%, a testament to tariff-related spending habits and the popularity of its latest devices, including a cheaper iPhone released earlier this year.

Amazon Shares Fall Because Cloud Unit's Growth Wasn't Enough for Wall Street

Amazon.com reported sharp increases in sales and profit, partly fueled by the growth of its cloud-computing business.

The company said Thursday that revenue rose by 13% during the second quarter to $168 billion. Profit increased 35%. The sales and profit results beat Wall Street's expectations.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Friday

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Financial Services Headlines at 12 AM ET: Berkshire Stock Sees Weird Trading. The A Shares Pop While B Shares Dip. | Wells ...
data.description:
Berkshire Stock Sees Weird Trading. The A Shares Pop While B Shares Dip.

It is unclear what caused the late-day buying in the A shares, but it looks like it will reverse Friday.

----

Wells Fargo to Name CEO Charlie Scharf Chairman

The appointment rewards the executive who led the bank back from a scandal involving the creation of millions of fake customer accounts.

----

Geico, Berkshire Hathaway's Car Insurer, Is on Recovery Road

Warren Buffett's 'first business love' needed some TLC, and tech and analytics investments have paid off. But Progressive still has the lead.

----

MicroStrategy Swings to Profit on Bitcoin Gains

The company's bitcoin per share increased by 25% in the second quarter from a year earlier.

----

Coinbase's Profit Jumps on Crypto Investment Gains

The largest U.S. crypto exchange's revenue fell short of Wall Street expectations.

----

Figma Is Largest VC-Backed American Tech Company IPO in Years

At more than $19 billion, the design-software company's initial market cap priced well above its private valuation, a welcome scenario for venture investors

----

Mastercard Profit Climbs on Revenue Gains From Spending, Services

Mastercard said more consumer spending in its payment network translated to higher revenue and earnings in the second quarter.

----

Intercontinental Exchange Revenue, Profit Increase

Intercontinental Exchange reported higher top- and bottom-line results in its latest quarter.

----

S&P Global Earnings Rise; Lowers FY Earnings Expectations

S&P Global said second-quarter profit rose due to "resilience in the debt and equity markets." However, the company lowered its full-year earnings view despite raising its expectations for revenue growth.

----

KKR Posts Higher Revenue, Increases Assets Under Management

The New York investment firm posted a profit of $472.4 million, compared with $667.9 million a year earlier.

----

Antares and Ascot Group Form New $500 Million Reinsurance Company

Insurers have taken a keen interest in private credit for higher yields and alignment of long-duration investments.

----

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Schroders, Just Group and more in the latest Market Talks covering Financial Services.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 12 AM ET: Chevron Is On a Roll Ahead of Second-Quarter Earnings | Exxon ...
data.description:
Chevron Is On a Roll Ahead of Second-Quarter Earnings

The energy explorer just closed the acquisition of Hess, and got permission from the Trump administration to resume operations in Venezuela.

----

Exxon Mobil Reports Earnings Friday. What's Holding the Stock Back.

The oil giant's earnings are set to fall from last year's levels.

----

First Solar Posts Earnings Beat, Lifts Revenue Outlook

The solar energy company posted second-quarter earnings above analysts' expectations and raised its 2025 revenue guidance.

----

Oil Futures Pull Back After Rally

Oil futures snapped a three-day winning streak with the approach of the U.S. tariff deadline and the OPEC+ meeting that's expected to bring another large output increase for September.

----

Major Banks Nudge Oil Forecast Higher But U.S. Tariffs, OPEC+ Hikes Weigh on Outlook

Major Wall Street banks have modestly lifted their oil-price forecasts for this year and next, but lingering uncertainty over U.S. tariffs and rising OPEC+ output continue to cloud the outlook.

----

Shell Keeps $3.5 Billion Buyback as Earnings Fall Less Than Expected

The oil major maintains the pace of shareholder returns despite lower prices weighing on energy companies' balance sheets.

----

Enel Launches $1.14 Billion Share Buyback, Confirms Guidance

The Rome-based energy company said first-half net profit fell 11% because the prior year's results were boosted by proceeds from its disposal plan.

----

MPLX to Buy Northwind Midstream for $2.38 Billion

The midstream energy-and-logistics business will gain PE-backed Northwind's portfolio of over 200,000 acres and more than 200 miles of gathering pipelines as part of the deal.

----

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Recover Ground

U.S. natural gas futures settled higher in a choppy session. A pickup in LNG feedgas demand and a still "quite hot" second week of August likely contributed to gains, NatGasWeather.com said.

----

Schneider Electric's Double-Digit Growth Driven by Data Center Deals

The company said it remains well placed to seize opportunities in its end markets, despite an environment of heightened uncertainty.

----

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on oil futures, Shell and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Post Large Weekly Build

U.S. crude oil inventories rose sharply last week as domestic production and imports increased and exports fell, according to data released by the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

data.title: India's City Union Bank gains after Q1 profit rise; Investec raises TP
data.description:
** Shares of City Union Bank rise 2% to 219 rupees

** Private lender posts a 15.6% Y/Y rise in June-qtr net profit at 3.06 bln rupees ($34.92 mln), while gross non-performing assets fall to 2.99% Y/Y from 3.88% Y/Y

** Investec raises TP target price to 280 rupees from 260, implying 30.7% upside from last close; maintains "buy" rating

** Raises FY26/27 NIM estimates by 8/4 bps; PAT forecasts up 4/6% (RoA: 1.55/1.57%), partly offset by higher credit costs

** Says CTBK is top pick for its steady RoA, strong micro-market hold, clear growth path, and prudent risk approach

** CTBK rated "strong buy" by 22 analysts on average; median target price is 213.5 rupees – data compiled by LSEG

** Stock up 26% YTD

($1 = 87.6400 Indian rupees)

data.title: Markets for fall for 2nd day: Sensex down 200 pts, Nifty below 24,750 as IT, metals decline
Hong Kong Growth Likely Stronger Than Previously Expected — Market Talk
data.description: Hong Kong's growth is likely to be stronger than previously expected after a solid 2Q print, Goldman Sachs economists say in a research note. The city's GDP expanded more than expected on an on-year basis in 2Q, mainly due to inventory restocking, GS notes. A better-than-expected start to the year and recovering consumption implies higher full-year growth. GS revises up its 2025 real GDP growth forecast to 2.6%, compared with 2.0% previously. They also raise their 2026 real GDP growth estimate to 1.8% from 1.7% previously. Markets await further GDP details, which are likely to be released mid-August. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Lupin shares fall 2.06% in early trade on Nifty Midcap 150, among top midcap losers
Bharti Airtel shares decline marginally in early trade
Ero Copper Corp reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Ero Copper Corp reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 46 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended June 30, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 18 cents. The mean expectation of twelve analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 41 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 28 cents to 67 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 39.6% to $163.51 million from a year ago; analysts expected $172.98 million.

  • Ero Copper Corp's reported EPS for the quarter was 68 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $70.55 million.

  • Ero Copper Corp shares had fallen by 21.8% this quarter and lost 7.2% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 24% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, four analysts negatively revised earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 13 "strong buy" or "buy," 2 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the specialty mining & metals peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Ero Copper Corp is C$26.25, about 28.6% above its last closing price of C$18.75

data.title: Malaysia Views U.S. Tariff Cut as Positive Outcome
data.description:
By Ying Xian Wong

KUALA LUMPUR, Malaysia--The U.S. decision to reduce tariffs on Malaysian exports to 19% from 25% is a positive outcome, and the government will work closely with relevant parties to mitigate the impact on exports, Malaysia's trade ministry said.

The outcome follows nearly three months of sustained engagement between the two countries, and the 19% rate brings Malaysia roughly in line with other Southeast Asian economies, the Ministry of Investment, Trade and Industry said Friday.

Malaysia stood firm on various "red line" items and achieved the rate without compromising on its sovereign right to implement key policies, MITI said, and views the lower rate as "a significant achievement of Malaysia's thorough and methodical negotiating process".

MITI will work with other ministries and agencies to manage the tariff impact on exporters, and push industrial reform under the country's development blueprints.

Despite global economic headwinds and rising geopolitical tensions, MITI said Malaysia's domestic demand remains robust and private consumption continues to support growth.

MITI will roll out outreach programs in the coming months to help exporters adjust to the revised tariff structure.

The U.S. is Malaysia's largest export destination, with shipments valued at 198.65 billion ringgit, equivalent to $46.58 billion, in 2024. It is also the top source of foreign investment, with 32.82 ringgit billion in approved investments last year.

Write to Ying Xian Wong at yingxian.wong@wsj.com

data.title: Train Stocks Could Fare Well as Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern Merge. Picking Winners. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Bill Alpert

When Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern announced their merger deal Tuesday to form "America's first transcontinental railroad," it puzzled some who recalled the golden spike driven in 1869 at Utah's Promontory Summit. That last spike only connected two railroads from Omaha, Neb., to Sacramento, Calif.

The proposed Union Pacific Transcontinental Railroad will link every corner of the country. And it may spur a second transcontinental network if Union Pacific's western rival, BNSF — a unit of Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway — feels forced to bid for Norfolk's eastern counterpart, CSX.

The resulting freight traffic could get rail stocks unstuck. That would be quite an achievement.

A combination of Union Pacific's western reach with Norfolk's East Coast network would cross 43 states and more than 50,000 miles. It would connect the ports of Seattle, Oakland, Calif., and Long Beach, Calif., with those in Houston, New Orleans, Norfolk, Va., and New York.

Union Pacific says a single train could take freight coast to coast without spending two days in Chicago shuffling cars between railroads. The time saved could let the railroad win freight business away from truckers — ending years of flat rail volumes and flat rail stock prices.

"We're going to cut a day or two off every transit time," Union Pacific CEO Jim Vena told listeners on a call to discuss the deal. He will run the combined company.

Though the deal will take two years to go through the approval process, there's a strong case that it could lift Union Pacific stock nearly 50% in the next five years.

Union Pacific stock has slipped 3.2%, to $222, since Tuesday's announcement. Norfolk has slid 2.9% to $278, which is $42 shy of the $320 announced value of the deal — a share of Norfolk swapped for a share of Union Pacific, plus $88.82 cash. That $42 spread implies that traders put a 60% probability of the deal going through.

The discount makes sense for now. The companies say it could take until early 2027 for the merger to win approval from shareholders and the Surface Transportation Board, or STB, that oversees the rail industry. Investors in short-term Treasuries could earn a guaranteed 5% in that time frame, while the deal could hit delays along the way.

The STB adopted rules in 2001 that raised high hurdles to large-scale rail mergers. Objections may come from shippers and from rivals such as CSX and BNSF. Unions have already criticized Union Pacific's labor and safety record.

But Vena is betting that Washington has gotten more comfortable with mergers. The STB's new chair, Patrick Fuchs, has scheduled meetings that may reassess the 2001 merger rules. And President Donald Trump can appoint a fifth board member to the STB, tilting the body that's now evenly split between Republicans and Democrats.

On the Tuesday call, Union Pacific and Norfolk executives contended that their combination would help U.S. port cities by winning away some of the "intermodal" container shipments now carried by Canadian National Railway and Canadian Pacific Kansas City.

Vena said the merger will improve service and expand options for shippers. The two companies currently hand off a million cars a year in Midwest rail yards. By eliminating that friction, rail freight will become faster, cheaper, and more competitive with the $400 billion trucking market.

"One intermodal train removes more than 550 trucks from the highway and is 75% more fuel efficient than trucks," Vena said.

The resulting company will also offer significant upside to today's shareholders, said Norfolk CEO Mark George. "[It] will be a must-own large-cap stock and should trade at a robust multiple."

He may be right. Rather than worrying about the spread between the two stocks, long-term investors should be weighing how Union Pacific's transcontinental railroad — and its rivals — will fare.

The terms of Union Pacific's deal value Norfolk at a generous 22-times estimates for Norfolk's 2026 earnings, handing over more Union Pacific stock in order to leave the combo with less debt. J.P. Morgan analysts figure the deal will add about $24 billion in debt to the $46 billion that the two railroads will have when the deal closes.

The combined 2024 revenue of the two railroads was $36.4 billion, with $18 billion in combined earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, or Ebitda. In a Wednesday note, J.P. Morgan analyst Brian Ossenbeck forecast that 2030 Ebitda could be $27 billion, with net profit of $16 billion, or $20 a share. Free cash flow would be $13.6 billion.

If an investor put a 13-times multiple on that 2030 Ebtida — comparable to what Union Pacific rates today — the enterprise value of the transcontinental railroad would be around $350 billion in 2030, compared with $245 billion for the two companies today. The 2030 market capitalization would be $280 billion, or some 47% above today's combined market caps.

Ossenbeck has Hold ratings on both Union Pacific and Norfolk, citing their long haul to a closed deal. But he thinks the transcontinental combo could generate substantial free cash.

"[Union Pacific] looks like it will be the most interesting way to play the first transcon railroad theme when the dust settles," Ossenbeck concludes.

Buffett's Berkshire may be having thoughts about CSX, so that its $100 billion investment in BNSF isn't left at a competitive disadvantage. Bloomberg reported Thursday that CSX had hired Goldman Sachs for advice on its future. In combination, those two railroads would rival the reach, revenue, and profit of a Union Pacific/Norfolk combo.

The J.P. Morgan analyst figures that could lead Berkshire to an offer of at least $43 a share for CSX, which currently trades for $35.50.

Write to Bill Alpert at william.alpert@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Japan's Itochu Q1 net profit up about 37%, misses forecasts
data.description:
Japanese trading house Itochu on Friday posted 283.94 billion yen ($1.89 billion) in net profit for the quarter ended on June 30, up 37.4% from the same period a year ago but missing analysts' forecasts.

An LSEG poll of analysts had expected Itochu to post 290.95 billion yen in net profit for the quarter. In the same period of last year, Itochu saw a net profit of 206.6 billion yen.

The company kept its full fiscal year net profit forecast unchanged at 900 billion yen.

($1 = 150.5700 yen)

data.title: Australia's Star Entertainment plunges on Brisbane casino deal uncertainty
data.description:
** Shares of Star Entertainment down 15.5% at A$0.093, as of 0356 GMT

** Earlier fell as much as 17.3% to record low of A$0.091, marking biggest intraday pct loss since Feb 28

** Casino operator reviewing options to sell 50% stake in Brisbane's new Queen's Wharf casino and hotel complex after Chow Tai Fook Enterprises and Far East Consortium declined to extend deadline

** SGR must now repay A$10 million ($6.44 million) by Aug 6 to the two companies after deal fails

** "Star's cash flow continues to wither and the back-and-forth over commitments only muddies the picture further," said John Lockton, head of investment strategy, MST Financial

** YTD, SGR down 50.5%

($1 = 1.5538 Australian dollars)

data.title: AGC Sees FY Net Y57.00B

Slow US job gains expected in July; unemployment rate forecast rising to 4.2%
data.description:
Key points:
  • Nonfarm payrolls forecast to increase by 110,000 in July
  • Unemployment rate expected to rise to 4.2% from 4.1% in June

U.S. job growth likely slowed in July, with the unemployment rate forecast rising back to 4.2%, but that probably would be insufficient to spur the Federal Reserve to resume cutting interest rates soon as tariffs are starting to fan inflation.

The anticipated slowdown in nonfarm payrolls in the Labor Department's closely watched employment report on Friday would mostly be payback after a surprise surge in state and local government education boosted employment gains in June.

The U.S. central bank on Wednesday left its benchmark interest rate in the 4.25%-4.50% range. Fed Chair Jerome Powell's comments after the decision undercut confidence the central bank would resume policy easing in September as had been widely anticipated by financial markets and some economists.

Though Powell described the labor market as being in balance because of supply and demand both declining at the same time, he acknowledged that this dynamic was "suggestive of downside risk." Job growth has slowed amid uncertainty over where President Donald Trump's tariff levels will eventually settle.

Trump on Thursday slapped dozens of trading partners with steep tariffs ahead of a Friday trade deal deadline, including a 35% duty on many goods from Canada.

The White House's immigration crackdown has reduced labor supply as has an acceleration of baby boomer retirements.

"We just don't have a roadmap yet with respect to tariffs, and now that it's coming into place, I think that can certainly help, but if you're thinking about what you're planning for your business over the next two to three years ... you don't want to make that decision until you know what your costs of running your business are going to be," said Michael Reid, senior U.S. economist at RBC Capital Markets.

Nonfarm payrolls likely increased by 110,000 jobs last month after rising by 147,000 in June, a Reuters survey of economists showed. That reading would be below the three-month average gain of 150,000. Estimates ranged from no jobs added to an increase of 176,000 positions. An economist predicting no change in payrolls pointed to the jump in state and local government education jobs in June, which accounted for nearly half of the employment gains that month.

"When the academic year ends, there is a huge drop in payroll levels at schools," said Stephen Stanley, chief U.S. economist at Santander U.S. Capital Markets. "The fact that there were fewer reductions than usual in June suggests to me that more of the usual wave of reductions came in July."

Stanley also argued that there had been a torrent of anecdotal and survey evidence suggesting that businesses large and small slowed their hiring activity this summer in the face of elevated policy uncertainty. This led Stanley to anticipate private sector payrolls growth slowed further in July rather than accelerated as most economists expected after the economy added the fewest jobs in eight months in June.

LOW BREAK-EVEN NUMBER

Federal government job losses as the Trump administration wields the axe on headcount and spending, excluding immigration enforcement, could mount after the Supreme Court gave the White House the green light for mass firings.

But the administration has also said several agencies were not planning to proceed with layoffs.

The reduction in immigration flows means the economy now needs to create roughly 100,000 jobs per month or less to keep up with growth in the working age population. The decline in the unemployment rate to 4.1% in June was in part due to people dropping out of the labor force. July's anticipated rise would still leave the jobless rate in the narrow 4.0%-4.2% range that has prevailed since May 2024.

"The July jobs report is unlikely to shake the Fed out of its 'wait-and-see' posture," said Gregory Daco, chief economist at EY-Parthenon. "But it will add further evidence that the labor market is gradually losing momentum."

Financial markets have pushed back an anticipated September rate cut to October. With tariffs starting to raise inflation, some economists believe the window for the Fed resuming policy easing this year is closing.

But others still believe the Fed could still cut rates in September, especially if the Bureau of Labor Statistics' preliminary payrolls benchmark revision in September projects a sharp decline in the employment level from April 2024 through March this year.

The Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages, derived from reports by employers to the state unemployment insurance programs, has indicated a much slower pace of job growth between April 2024 and December 2024 than payrolls have suggested.

"If it's an ugly downward revision, the Fed will move, there is no question," said Brian Bethune, an economics professor at Boston College.
